name: Untitled
prompt:
[HOOK — 0:00–0:20]

[Upbeat, direct-to-camera]

You've built your own income — but walk into a bank self-employed and the bar jumps: years of accounts, tax proof, paperwork — and you can still get a no. Harder? Yes. Impossible? No. Most rejections are people who didn't know the rules first.

This video breaks down what you need: documents, traps, tips — so you can get approved. New here? I'm from Double Ur Hustle — real finance for self-employed people. Subscribe. Let's go.

⸻

[SECTION 1 — WHO COUNTS AS SELF-EMPLOYED? — 0:20–0:44]

Lenders care about ownership, not how you describe yourself. In the UK you're usually "self-employed" if you own more than about 20–25% of a business and your income comes from it — sole traders, freelancers, contractors, directors, partners. If PAYE is your main income, even with a side hustle, you're often still "employed" — that can help. Umbrella company: six months of payslips, often not treated as self-employed — usually easier.

⸻

[SECTION 2 — HOW LENDERS ASSESS INCOME — 0:44–1:14]

Rules aren't the same everywhere. Sole trader / partnership: net profit over two to three years, averaged; a volatile year may pull you down to the lowest figure. Limited company: some lenders salary + dividends only; others add company profit after tax — huge swing in what you can borrow. I've seen £90k earned assessed at £38k on salary/dividends alone. Contractors: often day rate × five days × ~46 weeks. Borrowing: many cap near 4.5× annual income; some go higher for big earners.

⸻

[SECTION 3 — THE TAX TRAP — 1:14–1:40]

[Slightly more serious]

Low taxable income from good accounting? That's the same number lenders use to size your loan. You can't max tax optimisation and borrowing without a plan. If a mortgage is one to two years out, loop in you, your accountant, and a broker. Loading expenses right before you apply cuts profit — and your offer.

⸻

[SECTION 4 — TRADING HISTORY — 1:40–2:04]

Two to three years of accounts is the norm; two years is the mainstream sweet spot. One year? Narrower options — some lenders (e.g. Halifax-style packages) want extra statements and projections. Minimum is usually twelve months; under that is tough. Going sole trader → limited before applying can reset how some lenders see you — timing matters.

⸻

[SECTION 5 — DOCUMENTS — 2:04–2:32]

[Clear, organised]

Basics for everyone: ID, address, three to six months' bank statements, deposit proof. Sole trader: SA302 + Tax Year Overview — often two years. Limited company: certified accounts, accountant's certificate on salary, dividends, turnover, profit — accounts usually within 18 months. Contractor: day rate evidence, contracts, upcoming work if you have it. Messy files read as risk.

⸻

[SECTION 6 — MAXIMISE YOUR CHANCES — 2:32–2:58]

Credit file before you apply — fix errors, get on the electoral roll. Bigger deposit opens more doors (15%+ helps; short history may need 20%). Separate business and personal accounts. Tax paid and current. No new big credit right before applying — and every rejection leaves a trace; use a broker or research first.

⸻

[SECTION 7 — BROKER? — 2:58–3:20]

PAYE: maybe skip. Self-employed: almost always yes. Criteria differ by lender — years trading, company vs contractor, product fit. Whole-of-market brokers match you before hard searches and protect your score. Your bank only selling its own products limits you.

⸻

[OUTRO — 3:20–4:00]

Self-employed mortgages are messier — but doable if you know how lenders think, prep documents, and don't go in blind. Plan early, talk to your accountant, consider a broker who gets self-employment.

Like if this helped; subscribe to Double Ur Hustle for more. See you next time.

assets.name: Mortgage explainer
assets.description: UK finance YouTube explainer: 2D illustration with flat colour or soft cel-style shading — clear silhouettes, readable faces, broadcast-friendly contrast. Approachable and direct, not corporate-stiff. Not photoreal photography, not glossy stock-ad stills, and not cinematic live-action or documentary office B-roll unless the scene copy explicitly calls for it. One clear focal idea per frame; same graphic language as the square style-kit asset thumbnails (characters and props feel like they belong to one channel).

textStyles.instructions: Primary on-screen title: short, high impact, often a single claim or section title. Prefer one line; two only if both are short.
Supporting line: subtext, qualification, or CTA. Lighter weight than the title; use for subheads, caveats, and “what to do next”.

characters.name: Narrator
characters.description: The channel’s central host: direct-to-camera explainer energy like finance YouTube thumbnails—expressive face, simple outfit, the face of the story. He stands in for self-employed viewers learning the rules (charts, walkthroughs, reactions) but reads first as narrator/host, not only as an applicant. Smart-casual, approachable, not a caricature; arc from stressed or overwhelmed to calmer once things make sense.

characters.name: Lender
characters.description: Represents a bank or building society: professional, neutral, trustworthy. Explains rules in plain language—affordability, deposits, documents—not salesy. Typical props: branded folder, desk, screen with forms. Good for “what the bank needs from you” beats.

characters.name: Accountant
characters.description: Calm, numbers-first helper: reconciling books, SA302s, tax years, and declared income. Setting can be a small practice, home office, or meeting with the narrator. Laptop, printouts, calculator energy. Use when the story is about proving income from accounts, not just payslips.

characters.name: Broker
characters.description: Whole-of-market mortgage adviser: consultative, compares options, sits between the client and lenders. Often with a laptop, comparison notes, or a phone call in frame. Use for “shopping the market,” explaining product types, or walking through an application step-by-step.

characters.name: Employee
characters.description: A salaried PAYE worker for contrast: payslips, P60, simpler proof of income. Same friendly tone as the narrator but fewer moving parts—use in split-screen or before/after comparisons when you need to show how self-employed underwriting differs from standard employment.

scenes.title: Hook / Bank counter
scenes.description: Wide-to-medium finance-branch counter: the Narrator faces the viewer while the Lender stands behind glass or a low partition; between them, a tall stack of forms and folders with a small warning or alert icon so the self-employed scrutiny bar reads instantly. Energy is urgent but hooked—overwhelmed, still leaning in. Use this as the scene's authoritative reference still: one clear focal pile, readable faces, broadcast contrast so every later frame matches this staging and palette.
scenes.voiceoverText:
You've built your own income — but walk into a bank self-employed and the bar jumps: years of accounts, tax proof, paperwork — and you can still get a no. Harder? Yes. Impossible? No. Most rejections are people who didn't know the rules first.

This video breaks down what you need: documents, traps, tips — so you can get approved. New here? I'm from Double Ur Hustle — real finance for self-employed people. Subscribe. Let's go.

scenes.title: Who counts as self-employed?
scenes.description: Educational wide layout: the Narrator beside a large ownership graphic (pie or band highlighting roughly 20–25%) while the Lender points at the chart; the Employee appears in a secondary panel or stage left with payslip simplicity for PAYE contrast. Optional small umbrella or payslip stack hints at six-month treatment without readable text. Calm diagram-led energy—this keyframe should lock how the scene balances labels, equity, and employed versus self-employed paths.
scenes.voiceoverText: Lenders care about ownership, not how you describe yourself. In the UK you're usually "self-employed" if you own more than about 20–25% of a business and your income comes from it — sole traders, freelancers, contractors, directors, partners. If PAYE is your main income, even with a side hustle, you're often still "employed" — that can help. Umbrella company: six months of payslips, often not treated as self-employed — usually easier.

scenes.title: How lenders assess income
scenes.description: Analyst desk with Narrator and Lender over a screen or printout: rolling net-profit years for sole traders, a split treatment card for limited companies (salary plus dividends versus profit after tax), and a contractor strip with day-rate × week math. Add a borrowing band or multiplier graphic suggesting a typical income multiple cap. Medium shot with charts as hero props; this still defines the 'structure changes the maths' look for the entire section.
scenes.voiceoverText: Rules aren't the same everywhere. Sole trader / partnership: net profit over two to three years, averaged; a volatile year may pull you down to the lowest figure. Limited company: some lenders salary + dividends only; others add company profit after tax — huge swing in what you can borrow. I've seen £90k earned assessed at £38k on salary/dividends alone. Contractors: often day rate × five days × ~46 weeks. Borrowing: many cap near 4.5× annual income; some go higher for big earners.

scenes.title: The tax trap
scenes.description: Tighter office beat: the Accountant lays out an SA302 and tax-year summaries while the Narrator locks onto the figures—slightly serious, advisory not preachy. A simple balance or seesaw motif can imply tax optimisation versus borrowing power without legible captions. Paper-forward, warm desk light; this reference still is the caution beat where declared income is the same number lenders use to size the loan.
scenes.voiceoverText: Low taxable income from good accounting? That's the same number lenders use to size your loan. You can't max tax optimisation and borrowing without a plan. If a mortgage is one to two years out, loop in you, your accountant, and a broker. Loading expenses right before you apply cuts profit — and your offer.

scenes.title: Trading history timeline
scenes.description: Narrator and Lender share a horizontal timeline with markers at twelve months, two years, and three years of accounts; optional extra statements or projection cards hover near the one-year band. A branch or caution icon can flag sole-trader-to-limited switches that reset some lenders' clocks. Infographic clarity over drama; this keyframe sets how trading history and timing read across the whole scene.
scenes.voiceoverText: Two to three years of accounts is the norm; two years is the mainstream sweet spot. One year? Narrower options — some lenders (e.g. Halifax-style packages) want extra statements and projections. Minimum is usually twelve months; under that is tough. Going sole trader → limited before applying can reset how some lenders see you — timing matters.

scenes.title: The document pile
scenes.description: Tabletop hero: ID, bank statements, deposit proof, paired SA302s and Tax Year Overviews, certified accounts, accountant certificate, and contractor contracts grouped in neat labelled clusters—substantial but organised, not sloppy chaos. The Accountant arranges while the Narrator engages the viewer on why messy files read as risk. This still anchors the 'paperwork you actually bring' energy for every shot in the scene.
scenes.voiceoverText: Basics for everyone: ID, address, three to six months' bank statements, deposit proof. Sole trader: SA302 + Tax Year Overview — often two years. Limited company: certified accounts, accountant's certificate on salary, dividends, turnover, profit — accounts usually within 18 months. Contractor: day rate evidence, contracts, upcoming work if you have it. Messy files read as risk.

scenes.title: Maximising your chances
scenes.description: Single-host medium shot: the Narrator alone beside a credit-health dial or meter, electoral-roll check, stepped deposit tiers, and icons for separated business versus personal accounts—plus a clear 'pause' or slash on new credit before applying. Optimistic prep mood, not scare tactics. This reference still should read as the checklist beat that every frame in the scene inherits.
scenes.voiceoverText: Credit file before you apply — fix errors, get on the electoral roll. Bigger deposit opens more doors (15%+ helps; short history may need 20%). Separate business and personal accounts. Tax paid and current. No new big credit right before applying — and every rejection leaves a trace; use a broker or research first.

scenes.title: Whole-of-market broker
scenes.description: Consultation desk: the Broker's laptop shows a grid of lender logos or criteria columns with the Narrator leaning in—comparison arrows or a soft funnel suggest matching options before hard searches. Open, shop-the-market energy rather than a single bank poster. This keyframe is the visual contract for broker-led, whole-of-market staging across the scene.
scenes.voiceoverText: PAYE: maybe skip. Self-employed: almost always yes. Criteria differ by lender — years trading, company vs contractor, product fit. Whole-of-market brokers match you before hard searches and protect your score. Your bank only selling its own products limits you.

scenes.title: Outro
scenes.description: Warm medium close-up: the Narrator speaks straight to camera with a soft house or keys motif and a simple checkmark or completion icon in the background—closure and subscribe energy without literal on-screen text. Relaxed smile, static hero framing. This still is the relaxed end-card look target that every outro frame should match.
scenes.voiceoverText:
Self-employed mortgages are messier — but doable if you know how lenders think, prep documents, and don't go in blind. Plan early, talk to your accountant, consider a broker who gets self-employment.

Like if this helped; subscribe to Double Ur Hustle for more. See you next time.

frames.description: Wide establishing shot; narrator approaches the counter with upbeat energy.
Stack of paperwork rises between narrator and lender; the bar feels higher.
Lender leans in; warning icon pulses beside the document pile.
Narrator reacts — overwhelmed but hooked, leaning into the beat.
Direct-to-camera: rejections often mean you did not know the rules yet.
Channel ID and subscribe cue; hand gesture invites the viewer forward.
Ownership graphic animates in beside the narrator — it is about equity, not labels.
PAYE employee enters frame left for an instant contrast with payslip simplicity.
Lender points at the 20–25% ownership band on the on-screen graphic.
Split composition: self-employed path vs employed path, side by side.
Umbrella / six-month payslip note: often not classed as self-employed for lending.
Sole trader / partnership: net profit years roll up on the desk display.
Limited company: salary plus dividends vs profit-after-tax — two different ceilings.
Contractor strip: day rate × five × ~46 weeks animates across a calendar.
Borrowing band graphic: ~4.5× income cap with a higher-earner stretch callout.
Accountant unrolls the SA302; narrator locks onto the numbers.
Tax-versus-borrowing seesaw balances overhead — the trade-off lands.
Tighter framing: loading expenses before you apply cuts the offer — beat lands.
Timeline scrub: twelve months through three years of accounts on a rail.
One-year path: extra statements and income projection cards appear.
Caution card: sole trader to limited company can reset the clock — timing matters.
ID, bank statements, and deposit proof laid in a clean, organised grid.
Sole trader stack: SA302 and Tax Year Overview pairs labelled by tax year.
Limited company and contractor: certified accounts, certificate, and contracts fan out.
Credit meter and electoral roll checkmark — fix the file before you apply.
Deposit tiers and split accounts checklist; no new credit before applying.
Broker laptop grid: lender logos and criteria side by side.
Whole-of-market funnel vs single-bank limitation — protect the credit search.
Warm sign-off; like and subscribe marks settle — hold on the hero frame.
